Chapter 9 • Diagnostic imaging
ized dorsally, slightly caudal to the stomach, on lateral 
views. The urinary bladder, if not empty, should be visible 
in the dorsal abdomen. The ureters and urethra should not 
normally be visible. However, all areas of the urinary tract 
should be evaluated for any mineralization on both lateral 
and dorsoventral views (see Chapter 17).
Gastrointestinal contrast studies
Contrast studies of the gastrointestinal tract can be useful 
to help diagnose torsions, motility abnormalities and extra-
luminal lesions affecting gut function. 
Barium can be given orally by syringe, mixed with food 
if compliance is poor. Flavoured barium suspensions are 
available which may be more readily accepted by syringe. 
Barium can also be administered by nasogastric tube. A 
minimum dose of 15 ml per kilogram of bodyweight is 
needed to achieve diagnostic radiographs.
As in other species, a series of images are taken. The 
initial image is taken soon after giving the barium and should 
show the barium in the stomach. A second image 40 minutes 
later will normally show that the barium is entering the small 
intestines. At 2 hours the barium will be outlining the caecum 
in most individuals. It should be borne in mind that there is 
a lot of variation in normal guinea pigs and that many patho-
logical factors will influence gut motility.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
(a)
(a) Lateral and (b) dorsoventral radiographs of a boar. a = stomach;  
9.2
b = caecum; c = scrotum; d = urinary bladder; e = small intestines;  
f = heart; g = suprahamate process; h = trachea; i = angular process of the 
mandible; j = nasal turbinates; k = ethmoid turbinates; l = optic foramen;  
m = external acoustic meatus; n = oesophagus; o = lungs; p = liver.
Indications
Abdominal ultrasonography should be considered for 
guinea pigs with palpable abdominal masses or organ 
enlargement, abdominal pain, haematuria, weight loss, 
diarrhoea, abdominal distension, polyuria/polydipsia or 
suspected endocrine disease. In sows, it can also be used 
for pregnancy diagnosis and staging.
Ultrasonography can be used to assess the function 
of the heart and aid with the diagnosis of heart disease. 
It can also be used to examine the thyroid gland (Pignon 
and Mayer, 2013), intraocular structures and the retrobul-
bar space (Rajaei et al., 2016), in a similar way to that 
routinely performed with cats and dogs.
Besides being a tool for direct diagnosis, ultrasono-
graphy is also useful to guide:
■  Cystocentesis
■  Fine-needle aspiration of parenchymous organs or 
masses to provide samples for cytology (see 
Chapter 5)
■  Drainage of cavity or pericardial effusions (see 
Chapter 16)
■  Delivery of contrast media for pyelography
■  Flushing of ureteral uroliths (see Chapter 17).
Equipment
Equipment with high-frequency transducers (7.5–10 MHz, 
ideally higher) and probes with a small contact area should 
be used. For examinations such as echocardiography, 
equipment allowing a high frame rate or fast image update 
(b) is preferred. Doppler imaging can be very helpful to dif-
ferentiate blood vessels from other fluid-filled structures.
a non-invasive and safe tool that can be used on its own 
or in conjunction with other diagnostic techniques. The  Patient preparation

of adequate equipment. restrained in dorsal recumbency, so this position should

Guinea pigs should not be fasted before ultrasound 
examination. The target area should be clipped and 
cleaned, and ultrasound gel should be applied. Care should 
be taken to prevent hypothermia – for example, by limiting 
the size of the clipped area to that necessary for the 
examination and avoiding the use of alcohol-based anti-
septic solutions.
Abdomen
Guinea pigs possess a relatively large abdominal cavity 
with a well developed and complex digestive tract that 
occupies most of the abdomen (see Chapter 15). Because 
guinea pigs are hindgut fermenters, there is always a vari-
able amount of gas inside the stomach and intestines that 
can cause interference and obscure visualization of certain 
areas of the abdominal cavity.
The abdominal cavity should be checked for the pres-
ence of free fluid, which can be caused by organ failure, 
infection or neoplasia. If free fluid is present, its echogenic-
ity and general appearance (e.g. homogeneous, flocculent  Part of an intestinal section on longitudinal view, showing

graphically. Nevertheless, it is possible to check for  increased echogenicity of the contents and the presence

trichobezoars (Theus et al., 2008). These abnormal find-
ings are seldom specific and can be present in any patient 
with signs of hypomotility or gut stasis, so should always 
be considered within the patient’s clinical context.
Liver and gall bladder
The liver is best visualized in the cranial third of the abdo-
men with a ventral approach. It should be possible to visu-
alize the left medial and left lateral lobes and the papillary 
process on the left side of the patient. The right medial and 
right lateral lobes and the caudate process can be seen on 
the patient’s right side. The liver has a homogeneous thinly 
grainy echogenicity, similar to the spleen and slightly less 
echogenic than the renal cortex. The bile ducts are often 
difficult to visualize. The hepatic veins, caudal vena cava 
and branches of the portal vein can be seen as round 
anechoic structures (Figure 9.6). The use of colour Doppler 
can help to differentiate blood vessels from bile ducts. Ultrasonographically, the gall bladder has anechoic 
9.7
contents and sometimes appears as a bilobed structure. 
Normal liver parenchyma can be seen deep to the gall bladder.
Pancreas
The pancreas is difficult to visualize by ultrasonography 
in guinea pigs. It can be found caudal to the stomach and 
medial to the spleen. The normal pancreas should be 
slightly hyperechoic compared with the renal cortex and 
liver, and isoechoic to the surrounding adipose tissue, but 
the pancreas has no capsule and its limits are usually 
poorly defined.
Spleen and lymph nodes
The spleen has a triangular shape and can be found 
cranial to the left kidney and lateral to the greater curva-
ture of the stomach (Figure 9.8). A very thin hyperechoic 
capsule can be seen, and the parenchyma should be 
homogeneous, finely grainy and less echogenic than the 
renal cortex. The spleen should be examined for changes 
Ultrasonographic appearance of the normal liver,  in its size and echogenicity, as well as for the presence 
9.6
showing the left lateral and left medial lobes. The round  of nodules. The same pathologies seen in other mam-
anechoic structures are hepatic veins.
malian species, such as lymphoma, should be expected 
to be present in guinea pigs.
The liver should be assessed for its overall size, 
The mesenteric lymph nodes should be checked for 
changes in general or localized echogenicity, and the 
signs of reactivity, enlargement or changes in echogenicity 
presence of nodules or changes in vascularization. Hepatic 
changes include hepatomegaly, increased general echo-
genicity (suggestive of hepatic lipidosis), increased focal 
echogenicity or a nodular pattern (caused by neoplasia, 
granulomas or abscesses (Beregi et al., 2000)), decreased 
focal echogenicity (caused by some infiltrative diseases, 
such as lymphoma, or decreased blood supply), increased 
echogenicity of bile ducts (cholangiohepatitis) and dilated 
hepatic veins (hepatic congestion). Variable amounts of 
free abdominal fluid can occasionally be seen between 
the liver lobes with some hepatopathies. Ultrasonography 
can also aid in the collection of samples of liver paren-
chyma by fine-needle aspiration or biopsy in anaesthetized 
patients (Glińska-Suchocka et al., 2019).
The gall bladder can be found slightly to the left of the 
midline and is surrounded by the quadrate lobe of the liver. 
It has a thin hyperechoic wall, normally anechoic contents 
and a distal acoustic enhancement artefact. Its size and  Ultrasonographic appearance of the normal spleen.  
9.8
shape can vary significantly from one patient to another  Part of the stomach can also be seen deep to the spleen.

tasis, lymphadenitis). (usually incidental findings) (Figure 9.12); nephroliths or 
ureteroliths of variable size, with corresponding acoustic 
shadowing (Stieger et al., 2003); increased or decreased 
renal size; loss of corticomedullary differentiation; dilatation 
of the renal pelvis (Figure 9.13) or ureter (Figure 9.14); the 
presence of nodules; and hypoechoic areas of variable 
size. Many of these changes are not pathognomonic and 
should be considered in conjunction with the results of 
other diagnostic tests; nevertheless, they can aid with the 
diagnosis of chronic renal failure, pyelonephritis, renal 
neoplasia and urolithiasis. Ultrasound-guided fine-needle 
aspiration or biopsy of the kidneys in anaesthetized patients 
can also help in reaching a definitive diagnosis. Techniques 
for ultrasound-guided pyelography (d’Ovidio et al., 2020) 
and ultrasound-guided hydropropulsion of ureteroliths 
(Eshar et al., 2013) have also been described in guinea pigs.

9.13
The renal pelvis is dilated (apparent as a triangular   The adrenal glands are relatively large in guinea pigs and 
anechoic structure).
can be found craniomedial to each kidney. They are occa-
sionally difficult to visualize, especially the left adrenal 
gland. They are normally bilobed and hypoechoic relative 
to the renal cortex. Occasionally, generalized or local 
enlargement, or the presence of nodules on the adrenal 
glands, can be seen in patients with hyperadrenocorticism 
(Zaheer and Beaufrère, 2020).
Female reproductive tract
The ovaries can be seen dorsocaudal to each kidney, in 
direct contact with the cranial pole of the kidney; they 
normally appear as round structures with variable echo-
genicity. The uterus is usually only visible dorsal to the 
urinary bladder (Figure 9.17). In cross section it appears 
as a round to elliptical structure that is hypoechoic relative 
to the surrounding tissues. In pregnant guinea pigs, ultra-
sonography can be helpful to determine the stage of 
pregnancy and likely date of parturition (Figure 9.18).

(Adapted from Santos et al., 2014)
Sows frequently have ovarian cysts (Figure 9.19). These 
can be unilateral or bilateral, single or multiple, and vary 
greatly in size (Beregi et al., 1999). These structures can be 
incidental findings that are not causing any health problem, 
so their presence should be considered in the context of 
the patient’s clinical history and examination findings. 
Pathological findings in the cervix and uterine horns include 
distension, the presence of intraluminal fluid (suggestive of  9.20 The bladder (visible as a large structure with anechoic

tissue appears as more hyperechoic. The vesicular glands  useful for a general assessment of the heart and to look 
(seminal vesicles) lie caudodorsal to the bladder and can  for pericardial changes. M-mode allows measurement of 
the internal diameter of the chambers of the heart and the 
cardiac wall thickness. Colour Doppler allows assessment 
of the presence of regurgitation at the level of each heart 
valve. If available, pulse-wave spectral Doppler analysis 
can also be performed at the level of the aortic, pulmonary 
and mitral valves, to evaluate the velocity of the blood 
flow. The equipment used should include high-frequency 
probes (up to 12 MHz) with a small contact area and high 
frame rates (50–100 frames per second for two-dimen-
sional examination and >120 frames per second for 
M-mode). Reference values have been established for 
guinea pigs (see Chapter 16).
Guinea pigs have been extensively used as experi-
mental animal models for human cardiology and some 
types of heart disease have been described in the spe-
cies, namely cardiomyopathy, pericardial effusion, dys-
trophic mineralization of the myocardium and aorta, and 
rhabdomyomatosis. There are very few reports in the 
literature regarding the use of echocardiography in guinea 
pigs and most of them focus on the diagnosis and man-
agement of pericardial effusions (Dzyban et al., 2001; 
Quinton et al., 2014). The same principles of echocardi-

Computed tomography
CT uses X-ray imaging to produce three-dimensional 
Anaesthetized guinea pig positioned for computed 
images of body tissues. It is becoming increasingly com- 9.21
tomography of the skull.
monly used in veterinary medicine due to the falling costs 
of units (this is partly dependent on the type of CT; see 
below), the shorter time required to produce a scan and the 
greater versatility in imaging of tissues compared with MRI. 
CT is inferior to MRI for imaging the CNS and somewhat 
inferior for abdominal imaging (although imaging of the 
abdomen can be improved by the use of positive contrast), 
but superior for scanning bony tissues and the thorax.
Because it creates a three-dimensional image, CT is 
superior to conventional radiography, especially for areas 
where summation can be an issue, such as the skull and the 
pelvis. It is also capable of producing much greater detail 
than conventional radiography, especially for the chest and

Chapter 9 • Diagnostic imaging
Skull
CT is excellent for visualizing dental root disease and 
abscesses (see Chapter 10). Many other skull diseases 
may be linked to or coincidental to dental disease, so CT 
is the imaging modality of choice whenever dental or skull 
disease is suspected. CT is distinctly advantageous for 
the following conditions:
■  Upper respiratory infections – in particular where 
there is rhinitis or involvement of the paranasal 
sinuses. CT enables excellent visualization of the 
turbinate bones and any alterations in their struc-
ture or thickening of the mucosa (Figure 9.24). 
Nasal foreign bodies are rare in guinea pigs but may 
also be visualized via CT if they are present. CT is 
the tool of choice for investigating sinus disease, as 
the presence of secretions or discharges can be 
clearly seen with this modality (Figure 9.25). Any 
surgical planning related to opening or draining the 
sinuses will require CT imaging first
■  Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disease – the 
TMJs of guinea pigs are capable of a range of 
movements during mastication. They may also 
be traumatized (especially during dental 
procedures as a result of over-extension with 
dental gags) or become infected (septic arthri-

Interpretation of CT images requires both an under-

to liaise and work with a more specialized radiologist in 
interpreting CT images.
In basic terms, interpretation of CT images is similar to 
that of conventional radiographs in that bone/calcification 
appears bright (white) and soft tissue appears grey. How-
ever, in CT images both air and water appear dark (black). 
The radiodensity of the different tissues is important in 
assessing pathologies. These densities are expressed as 
Hounsfield units (which describe radiodensity in relation 
to that of water). They are not fully described for the normal 
guinea pig but can be extrapolated from other species, 
although some care should accordingly be taken when 
interpreting these densities.
Specific areas
This section is not intended as a complete guide to CT 
of the guinea pig. Rather, it highlights areas where CT 
holds advantages over conventional radiography and, 
hence, where the greater cost of CT may be more easily

Chapter 9 • Diagnostic imaging
Arthritis (especially osteoarthritis) is common in guinea 
pigs. Both CT and conventional radiography are suitable 
for imaging the majority of the joints. However, in regions 
where there is often summation, CT has considerable 
advantages, especially for the shoulders (Figure 9.34) and 
cervicothoracic spine (Figure 9.35).

Chapter 9 • Diagnostic imaging
9.38 Sagittal CT slice showing cardiomegaly in a guinea pig.
Abdomen
CT has considerable advantages over conventional radi-
ography in distinguishing the soft tissue structures of the 
abdomen, especially if used with intravenous positive 
contrast medium. The assessment of relative tissue densi-
ties (e.g. when diagnosing hepatic lipidosis) is much better 
with CT. Even without the use of contrast, certain struc-
tures are visualized more easily by CT, especially the 
kidneys and urinary tract (Figures 9.39–9.42). Visualization 
of calcified material in the urinary tract is possible using 
either CT or radiography.
Conventional radiography is quicker than CT and is 
useful for assessing gas patterns in cases of acute ileus, 
intestinal obstruction or gastric torsion. However, in more 
chronic cases, contrast CT may be more useful for assess-
Dorsal CT slice of the abdomen and thorax of a guinea
